subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 15, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Effect of Early Administration of Shenfu Injection on Hemodyna
sentences: Story 1 is about Effect of Early Administration of Shenfu Injection on Hemodynamics in Septic Patients: A Multicenter, Randomized, Controlled Trial..

11.6%; p = 0.04) and 28 days after treatment (15.1% vs.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: This is relevant to default ICU fluid choice; focus on absolute mortality difference, kidney outcomes, and whether the enrolled population matches your resuscitation patients.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Octenidine or Sterile Water Cleansing and Late-Onset Sepsis in
sentences: Story 2 is about Octenidine or Sterile Water Cleansing and Late-Onset Sepsis in Neonates in the NICU: A Randomized Clinical Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in ICUs of a tertiary care hospital in India between September 1, 2023, and August 31, 2025, how should we interpret l trial, daily skin cleansing with octenidine wipes showed no evidence of reducing the incidence of LOS in NICU neonates compared with sterile water, suggesting that infection prevention strategies should focus on multimodal interventions rather than rely?

Of 530 neonates eligible for intention-to-treat analysis (299 males [56.4%]; median gestational age, 35.0 weeks [IQR, 32.0-37.0 weeks]), LOS occurred in 39 of 266 (14.7%) in the octenidine group and 34 of 264 (12.9%) in the sterile water group (RR, 1.10; 95% CI, 0.75-1.70; P = .60).
Time to LOS was similar between groups (HR, 1.11; 95% CI, 0.71-1.78; P = .63).

Secondary outcomes for the octenidine vs sterile water interventions were similar, including LOS by 7 days (24 neonates [9.0%] vs 23 [8.7%]) and 14 days (15 [5.6%] vs 11 [4.2%]), all-cause mortality (12 [4.5%] vs 5 [1.9%]), and length of NICU stay in days (median, 11.0 [IQR, 6.0-21.0] vs 10.0 [IQR, 6.0-19.0]).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - International Validation of Temperature-Trajectory Sepsis Subp
sentences: Story 3 is about International Validation of Temperature-Trajectory Sepsis Subphenotypes With Longitudinal Immune and Coagulation Patterns and Its Implications for Immunoglobulin Therapy..
This is a recent research article from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

In total, 2478 patients were included and classified into four subphenotypes: hyperthermic slow resolvers (567; 23%), hyperthermic fast resolvers (397; 16%), normothermic (780; 31%), and hypothermic (HT, 734; 30%).
The HT subphenotype exhibited the highest mortality rate (25%), consistent with previous findings.

Immunoglobulin therapy showed heterogeneous effects across different subphenotypes, with patients in the HT subphenotype showing a consistent direction of benefit (primary subtyping: hazard ratio, 0.47; p = 0.03).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Effect of Early Administration of Shenfu Injection on Hemodynamics in Septic Patients: A Multicenter, Randomized, Controlled Trial.
articles.summary: Eligible septic patients were randomized to the SFI group or the control group, receiving either SFI or normal saline at a rate of 20 mL/hr in 5 hours from the time of sepsis diagnosis, bid for 3 consecutive days. A total of 172 patients were enrolled, with 86 assigned to the SFI group and 86 to the control group. However, compared with the control group, the SFI group showed significantly lower mortality both during ICU stay (3.5% vs. 11.6%; p = 0.04) and 28 days after treatment (15.1% vs. To evaluate the effect of early administration of Shenfu injection (SFI) on hemodynamics and clinical outcomes in ICU patients with sepsis.

articles.title: Octenidine or Sterile Water Cleansing and Late-Onset Sepsis in Neonates in the NICU: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
articles.summary: All neonates admitted to the NICU within 48 hours of birth without early-onset sepsis or preexisting skin lesions and not discharged from the NICU before 72 hours were included. Neonates were assessed and randomly assigned to receive once-daily full-body cleansing with either 0.1% octenidine dihydrochloride antimicrobial wipes or sterile water applied with sterile gauze until discharge, transfer to another facility, or death. Separate wipes or gauze were used for the upper and lower body, with genital cleansing performed last. The primary outcome was LOS rates (occurring >72 hours after birth through 28 days of life) among neonates cleansed with octenidine wipes vs with sterile water until discharge or transfer to the maternal bedside, assessed using relative risk (RR). Time to LOS was compared using the hazard ratio (HR).
articles.analysis: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

pico.outcomes: The primary outcome was LOS rates (occurring >72 hours after birth through 28 days of life) among neonates cleansed with octenidine wipes vs with sterile water until discharge or transfer to the maternal bedside, assessed using relative risk (RR). Time to LOS was compared using the hazard ratio (HR). All cases were independently adjudicated by 2 blinded...

keyResults: Of 530 neonates eligible for intention-to-treat analysis (299 males [56.4%]; median gestational age, 35.0 weeks [IQR, 32.0-37.0 weeks]), LOS occurred in 39 of 266 (14.7%) in the octenidine group and 34 of 264 (12.9%) in the sterile water group (RR, 1.10; 95% CI, 0.75-1.70; P = .60).
Time to LOS was similar between groups (HR, 1.11; 95% CI, 0.71-1.78; P = .63).

Secondary outcomes for the octenidine vs sterile water interventions were similar, including LOS by 7 days (24 neonates [9.0%] vs 23 [8.7%]) and 14 days (15 [5.6%] vs 11 [4.2%]), all-cause mortality (12 [4.5%] vs 5 [1.9%]), and length of NICU stay in days (median, 11.0 [IQR, 6.0-21.0] vs 10.0 [IQR, 6.0-19.0]).
Adverse skin reactions occurred in 15 neonates (5.6%) in the octenidine group and 16 (6.1%) in the sterile water group (RR, 1.10; 95% CI, 0.55-2.10).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.

articles.title: International Validation of Temperature-Trajectory Sepsis Subphenotypes With Longitudinal Immune and Coagulation Patterns and Its Implications for Immunoglobulin Therapy.
articles.summary: In total, 2478 patients were included and classified into four subphenotypes: hyperthermic slow resolvers (567; 23%), hyperthermic fast resolvers (397; 16%), normothermic (780; 31%), and hypothermic (HT, 734; 30%). The HT subphenotype exhibited the highest mortality rate (25%), consistent with previous findings. Immunoglobulin therapy showed heterogeneous effects across different subphenotypes, with patients in the HT subphenotype showing a consistent direction of benefit (primary subtyping: hazard ratio, 0.47; p = 0.03). Sepsis remains a major challenge in the ICU. Given the limitations of the one-size-fits-all strategy, precision medicine approaches are needed to identify distinct sepsis subtypes that may respond to different treatments.

pico.outcomes: Clinical and microbiological characteristics were compared across subphenotypes, and the interaction between subphenotype and immunoglobulin therapy on 30-day mortality was assessed. Immunoglobulin therapy showed heterogeneous effects across different subphenotypes, with patients in the HT subphenotype showing a consistent direction of benefit (primary subtyping: hazard ratio, 0.47; p = 0.03).
Clinical and microbiological characteristics were compared across subphenotypes, and the interaction between subphenotype and immunoglobulin therapy on 30-day mortality was assessed.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
